summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the Glances REST API returns CORS headers allowing any origin with credentials. The Access-Control-Allow-Origin header reflects the request Origin, and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials is set to true, enabling cross-origin authenticated data exfiltration from any website.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause the Glances web server to start and return CORS headers that reflect the request Origin with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, confirming that any website can make authenticated cross-origin requests.
actual_observed_result: Running the reproduction confirmed that the Glances web server returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin reflecting the request Origin header and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true on all API responses. This validates that the CORS configuration allows any origin to make credentialed cross-origin requests.
verification_method: The verify.sh script starts the Glances web server and sends requests with a custom Origin header to verify that the response includes Access-Control-Allow-Origin reflecting the provided origin and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. It distinguishes the vulnerable CORS configuration from a secure one that would not reflect arbitrary origins with credentials.
evidence_produced_by_run: Requests with Origin: http://evil.example.com received Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://evil.example.com in the response.
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true was present in the response, confirming credentials are allowed cross-origin.
The CORS headers were returned on all tested API endpoints (/api/4/config, /api/4/args, /api/4/status).
The server started with the default CORS configuration (allow_origins=['*'], allow_credentials=True).

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit or ref, and mounts that checkout into the container.
